questions.q: Per the channel, you should pick a model by:
choices: The loop your workload runs in
questions.explain: Pick by loop, not by model. A great model inside the wrong loop is still the wrong tool.

questions.q: For coding all day in production, the recommended tier pattern is:
choices: Tiered routing — Tier 2 default, Tier 1 on hard sub-tasks
questions.explain: Tiered routing: Sonnet default for routine work, Opus escalated for the hardest 10–20% of sub-tasks.

questions.q: Short answer: name one trigger that should make you escalate from Tier 2 to Tier 1.
questions.explain: Any of: three or more retries with the same prompt; the task escalates from routine to novel; production-critical work where reliability outweighs cost.

questions.q: Which benchmark does the channel flag as unreliable?
questions.explain: SWE-bench Pro is cherry-picked. Trust FrontierCoding Diamond, Artificial Analysis, and hands-on real-task tests.

questions.q: The biggest mistake beginners make when choosing tiers is:
choices: Picking by marketing or popularity instead of workload fit
questions.explain: Pick by loop, not by hype. Marketing and popularity correlate with capability, not with workload fit.

questions.q: How often does the channel recommend re-evaluating your tier choice?
choices: Quarterly, or when a major model release ships
Never — pick once and stick with it forever
questions.explain: Quarterly, or sooner if a major release lands. Tier lists go stale fast — your workload doesn't.
